narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection from law school stings—pouring your heart into applications only to face a tough prediction of a 61% chance of not passing the bar exam feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why the system saw things this way, like sharing a story from someone who's been in your shoes. Picture the admissions model as an experienced mentor reviewing countless paths to the bar, and for you, it zeroed in first on your Year 3 decile of 2, that low ranking among peers that stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward struggle ahead. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 25, which didn't quite shine in the way that usually lights up successful trajectories, making the model worry about the hurdles in law's high-stakes testing world. Your Year 1 decile of 2 echoed this early pattern, suggesting consistency in a tougher academic lane rather than a climb out of it. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model linked to added pressures without the buffers higher resources sometimes provide for bar prep marathons. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 fit into this narrative too, painting a picture of steady but not standout performance coming in. Even elements like being a white male opting for full-time studies nudged the story toward higher risk in the model's eyes, based on patterns it's seen before. It wasn't one thing alone, but this combination—like chapters in a book that all hinted at an uphill battle—led to the call that your odds tipped over into failure territory more often than not. You're more than these numbers, though; many have rewritten their stories from here with targeted boosts in study habits or test prep. This prediction is just a snapshot, not your destiny—consider retaking the LSAT or building that Year 3 momentum elsewhere. You've got resilience in you; channel it, and doors will open wider. Hang in there.
